title: Electric potential: definition
topic_path: Electric potential energy, electric potential, and voltage

assessment_items.question_content:
A positive charge, $Q$, is located at point $\text O$.

[[☃ image 1]]

An external force does $3\ \text J$ of work in slowly pushing a point charge (no acceleration), $q=-2\ \text C$, from point $\text A$ to point $\text B$.


**What is the potential difference, $\text V_\text A-\text V_\text B$, or, $\text V_\text{AB}$?**    

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Work done in moving the charge from $\text A$ to $\text B$, $W_{\text{AB}}$ $=$ the potential energy gained or lost (when the charge reaches $\text B$), $\Delta U=U_\text B-U_\text A$. 

Let's recall, when a charge, $q'$, is moved in an electric field between two points, the *change in potential energy per unit charge* is defined as the **potential difference** between those points, or,

$\Delta V=\dfrac{\Delta U}{q'}$

##Solution

- $W_\text{AB}=3\ \text J$,

- $q=-2\ \text C$.

[[☃ image 1]]

$\begin{align}
V_\text{AB}&=V_\text A-V_\text B\\\\
&=\dfrac{U_\text A-U_\text B}{q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{U_\text B-U_\text A}}{\blueE q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{W_\text{AB}}}{\blueE q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{3\ \text J}}{\blueE{-2\ \text C}}\\\\
&=1.5\ \dfrac{\text J}{\text C}\\\\
\Rightarrow V_\text{AB}&=1.5\ \text V
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$1.5\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
A positive charge, $Q$, is located at point $\text O$.

[[☃ image 1]]

An external force does $2\ \text J$ of work in slowly pushing a point charge (no acceleration), $q=0.5\ \text C$, from point $\text B$ to point $\text A$.


**What is the potential difference, $\text V_\text A-\text V_\text B$, or, $\text V_\text{AB}$?**    

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Work done in moving the charge from $\text B$ to $\text A$, $W_{\text{BA}}$ $=$ the potential energy gained or lost (when the charge reaches $\text A$), $\Delta U=U_\text A-U_\text B$. 

Let's recall, when a charge, $q'$, is moved in an electric field between two points, the *change in potential energy per unit charge* is defined as the **potential difference** between those points, or,

$\Delta V=\dfrac{\Delta U}{q'}$

##Solution

- $W_\text{BA}=2\ \text J$,

- $q=0.5\ \text C$.

[[☃ image 2]]

$\begin{align}
V_\text{AB}&=V_\text A-V_\text B\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redE{U_\text A-U_\text B}}{q}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redE{W_\text{BA}}}{q}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redE{2\ \text J}}{\blueE{0.5\ \text C}}\\\\
&=4\ \dfrac{\text J}{\text C}\\\\
\Rightarrow V_\text{AB}&=4\ \text V
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$4\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
A positive charge, $Q$, is located at point $\text O$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The electrostatic force due to $Q$, slowly pushes a point charge (no acceleration), $q=3\ \text C$, from point $\text A$ to point $\text B$, doing $1.5\ \text J$ of work.

**What is the potential difference, $\text V_\text B-\text V_\text A$, or, $\text V_\text{BA}$?**    

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Solution

- Here, work done by the electric force $\redE {\left(W_{el}\right)_{\text A \rightarrow \text B}}=1.5\ \text J$,

- Charge being moved, $\blueE q=3\ \text C$.

[[☃ image 2]]
Now,

$\begin{align}
V_\text{BA}&=V_\text B-V_\text A\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redE{U_\text B-U_\text A}}{\blueE q}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redE{-\redE {\left(W_{el}\right)_{\text A \rightarrow \text B}}}}{\blueE q}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redE{-1.5\ \text J}}{\blueE{3\ \text C}}\\\\
&=-0.5\ \dfrac{\text J}{\text C}\\\\
\Rightarrow V_\text{BA}&=-0.5\ \text V
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$-0.5\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
A positive charge, $Q$, is located at point $\text O$.

[[☃ image 1]]

The electrostatic force due to $Q$, slowly pulls a point charge (no acceleration), $q=-1.5\ \text C$, from point $\text B$ to point $\text A$, doing $3\ \text J$ of work.

**What is the potential difference, $\text V_\text B-\text V_\text A$, or, $\text V_\text{BA}$?**    

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Solution

- Here, work done by the electric force $\redE {\left(W_{el}\right)_{\text B \rightarrow \text A}}=3\ \text J$,


- Charge being moved, $\blueE q=-1.5\ \text C$.

[[☃ image 2]]

$\begin{align}
V_\text{BA}&=V_\text B-V_\text A\\\\
&=\dfrac{{U_\text B-U_\text A}}{q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{U_\text A-U_\text B}}{\blueE q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE  {-\left(W_{el}\right)_{\text B \rightarrow \text A}}}{\blueE q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{\cancel{-}\left(3\ \text J\right)}}{\blueE{\cancel{-}1.5\ \text C}}\\\\
&=-2\ \dfrac{\text J}{\text C}\\\\
\Rightarrow V_\text{BA}&=-2\ \text V
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$-2\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
A negative charge, $Q$, is located at point $\text O$.

[[☃ image 1]]

A point charge, $q=2.5\ \text C$, is slowly moved (no acceleration) from point $\text A$ to point $\text B$. The point charge gains $2\ \text J$ in potential energy.

**What is the potential difference, $\text V_\text A-\text V_\text B$, or, $\text V_\text{AB}$?**    

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Let's recall, when a charge, $q'$, is displaced in an electric field between two points, the *change in potential energy per unit charge* is defined as the **potential difference** between those points, or,

$\Delta V=\dfrac{\Delta U}{q'}$

##Solution

- $\Delta U=U_\text B-U_\text A=2\ \text J$,

- $q=2.5\ \text C$.

[[☃ image 3]]

$\begin{align}
V_\text{AB}&=V_\text A-V_\text B\\\\
&=\dfrac{{U_\text A-U_\text B}}{q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{U_\text B-U_\text A}}{\blueE q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{2\ \text J}}{\blueE{2.5\ \text C}}\\\\
&=-0.8\ \dfrac{\text J}{\text C}\\\\
\Rightarrow V_\text{AB}&=-0.8\ \text V
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$-0.8\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
A negative charge, $Q$, is located at point $\text O$.

[[☃ image 1]]

A point charge, $q=-2.5\ \text C$, is slowly moved (no acceleration) from point $\text B$ to point $\text A$. The point charge gains $1\ \text J$ in potential energy.

**What is the potential difference, $\text V_\text A-\text V_\text B$, or, $\text V_\text{AB}$?**    

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Let's recall, when a charge, $q'$, is displaced in an electric field between two points, the *change in potential energy per unit charge* is defined as the **potential difference** between those points, or,

$\Delta V=\dfrac{\Delta U}{q'}$

##Solution

- $\Delta U=U_\text A-U_\text B=1\ \text J$,

- $q=-2.5\ \text C$.

[[☃ image 3]]

$\begin{align}
V_\text{AB}&=V_\text A-V_\text B\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redE{U_\text A-U_\text B}}{\blueE q}\\\\
&=\dfrac{\redE{1\ \text J}}{\blueE{-2.5\ \text C}}\\\\
&=-0.4\ \dfrac{\text J}{\text C}\\\\
\Rightarrow V_\text{AB}&=-0.4\ \text V
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$-0.4\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
A negative charge, $Q$, is located at point $\text O$.

[[☃ image 1]]

A point charge, $q=-4\ \text C$, loses $1.6\ \text J$ in potential energy as it's moved slowly (no acceleration) from point $\text A$ to infinity.

**What is the potential at point $\text A$, $V_\text A$?**    

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Let's recall, when a charge, $q'$, is displaced in an electric field between two points, the *change in potential energy per unit charge* is defined as the **potential difference** between those points, or,

$\Delta V=\dfrac{\Delta U}{q'}$

- In this problem, the potential energy of the point charge at infinity is $1.6\ \text J$ *less* compared to point $\text A$, or, $U_\infty-U_\text A=-1.6\ \text J$.

- The potential energy is taken to be zero at infinity.

##Solution

- $U_\infty-U_\text A=-1.6\ \text J$,

- $q=-4\ \text C$.

The potential at $\text A$, $V_\text A$, equals the potential difference, $V_\text A-V_\infty$, by definition. We have,

$\begin{align}
V_\text A&=V_\text A-V_\infty\\\\
&=\dfrac{U_\text A-U_\infty}{q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{U_\infty-U_\text A}}{\blueE q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{-1.6\ \text J}}{\blueE{-4\ \text C}}\\\\
&=-0.4\ \dfrac{\text J}{\text C}\\\\
\Rightarrow V_\text A&=-0.4\ \text V
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$-0.4\ \text V$

assessment_items.question_content:
A negative charge, $Q$, is located at point $\text O$.

[[☃ image 1]]

A point charge, $q=1.2\ \text C$, gains $3\ \text J$ in potential energy as it's moved slowly (no acceleration) from point $\text A$ to infinity.

**What is the potential at point $\text A$, $V_\text A$?**    

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
##Strategy

Let's recall, when a charge, $q'$, is displaced in an electric field between two points, the *change in potential energy per unit charge* is defined as the **potential difference** between those points, or,

$\Delta V=\dfrac{\Delta U}{q'}$

- In this problem, the potential energy of the point charge at infinity is $3\ \text J$ *more* compared to point $\text A$, or, $U_\infty-U_\text A=3\ \text J$.

- The potential energy is taken to be zero at infinity.

##Solution

- $U_\infty-U_\text A=3\ \text J$,

- $q=1.2\ \text C$.

The potential at $\text A$, $V_\text A$, equals the potential difference, $V_\text A-V_\infty$, by definition. We have,

$\begin{align}
V_\text A&=V_\text A-V_\infty\\\\
&=\dfrac{U_\text A-U_\infty}{q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{U_\infty-U_\text A}}{\blueE q}\\\\
&=-\dfrac{\redE{3\ \text J}}{\blueE{1.2\ \text C}}\\\\
&=-2.5\ \dfrac{\text J}{\text C}\\\\
\Rightarrow V_\text A&=-2.5\ \text V
\end{align}$

The correct answer is:

$-2.5\ \text V$
